title: Introducing Formantis
text: Formantis is an acoustic modeling engine that converts raw audio into phoneme and word hypotheses for automatic speech recognition, licensed by voice-assistant and transcription vendors who need a production-grade acoustic model without training one from scratch on their own audio corpus. The engine covers the full acoustic pipeline from waveform to decoded hypothesis: feature extraction, a neural acoustic model that scores phoneme and subword units against those features, and a beam-search decoder that combines acoustic scores with an external language model to produce a final transcript. Formantis ships pretrained models for eleven languages as of the current release, alongside a fine-tuning toolkit that lets a licensee adapt the base model to domain-specific vocabulary, such as medical dictation or call-center terminology, using a comparatively small amount of labeled in-domain audio rather than a full retraining run.

title: Feature Extraction Pipeline
text: Formantis extracts an 80-channel log-mel spectrogram from each 25-millisecond audio frame, computed with a 10-millisecond frame shift so consecutive frames overlap by 15 milliseconds, a standard configuration chosen to balance temporal resolution against the added computation of a shorter shift. Each frame is windowed, transformed via FFT, passed through a mel-scale filterbank that concentrates resolution in the frequency ranges most relevant to human speech perception, and log-compressed before being stacked into the spectrogram the acoustic model consumes directly. Formantis deliberately feeds the acoustic model log-mel spectrograms rather than classical MFCC coefficients, since the model's own early convolutional layers learn a decorrelating transform comparable to the discrete cosine transform MFCC extraction applies by hand, making that hand-designed step unnecessary for a model trained end to end. Feature extraction runs in under 3 milliseconds per second of audio on the reference deployment hardware.

title: Acoustic Model Architecture
text: Formantis's acoustic model is a Conformer encoder, combining convolutional layers that capture local acoustic patterns with self-attention layers that capture longer-range context across an utterance, paired with a transducer decoder that predicts output tokens incrementally rather than requiring the full utterance before producing any output. This streaming-capable design lets Formantis begin emitting partial hypotheses well before an utterance finishes, a requirement for voice-assistant licensees needing low-latency response rather than only offline transcription accuracy. The encoder operates on subword units derived from a language-specific byte-pair-encoding vocabulary rather than raw phonemes directly, though Formantis exposes a separate phoneme-alignment output alongside the subword transcript for licensees whose downstream application, such as pronunciation scoring, specifically needs phoneme-level timing rather than just the final word sequence.

title: Model Size and Parameter Specifications
text: Formantis ships three model sizes: Compact at 28 million parameters, Standard at 140 million parameters, and Large at 610 million parameters, all sharing the same Conformer-transducer architecture at different layer counts and hidden dimensions. The Compact model uses 8 Conformer blocks with a 256-dimensional hidden size, Standard uses 16 blocks at 512 dimensions, and Large uses 24 blocks at 768 dimensions. All three variants share an identical 4,096-token subword vocabulary per language, trained jointly across the model family so a licensee can switch model size without retraining the tokenizer. Large model inference requires a minimum of 2.1 gigabytes of accelerator memory at batch size one, while Compact runs within 340 megabytes, a figure that determines which model size is practical on-device versus server-side for a given licensee's hardware budget.

title: Word Error Rate Benchmark Statistics
text: On Formantis's internal English benchmark set, drawn from a mix of conversational and read-speech recordings, the Large model achieves a word error rate of 4.1%, Standard achieves 5.8%, and Compact achieves 8.9%, measured with the standard beam-search decoder and a fused trigram language model. Under high-noise conditions, simulated by adding babble noise at 10 decibels signal-to-noise ratio, word error rate rises to 9.7% for Large, 13.2% for Standard, and 19.4% for Compact, reflecting the smaller models' comparatively reduced robustness to acoustic degradation. Across Formantis's ten non-English language releases, mean word error rate on each language's internal benchmark is 6.3% for the Large model, though individual languages range from 4.4% for the best-covered language to 11.8% for the language with the smallest training corpus.

title: Word Error Rate by Model Size and Release
text:
Word error rate has improved across three major Formantis releases as training data volume and model architecture both matured.

| Release | Year | Large WER | Standard WER | Compact WER |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| v1.0 | 2021 | 7.8% | 10.4% | 15.1% |
| v2.0 | 2023 | 5.4% | 7.6% | 11.2% |
| v3.0 | 2025 | 4.1% | 5.8% | 8.9% |

The v2.0-to-v3.0 improvement traces mainly to a training-data expansion rather than an architecture change, since the Conformer-transducer architecture itself has remained stable since v2.0's release.

title: Word Error Rate and Beam Search Formulas
text: Formantis computes word error rate using the standard alignment-based formula WER = (S + D + I) / N, where S is substitutions, D is deletions, I is insertions, and N is the total word count in the reference transcript, all counted via a minimum-edit-distance alignment between hypothesis and reference. During decoding, the beam-search decoder retains the top-k partial hypotheses at each decoding step, scoring each by log P(acoustic) + lambda * log P(language-model), where lambda is a tunable weight controlling how heavily the external language model influences the final hypothesis relative to the acoustic model's own scores. Formantis's default beam width is 16 and default lambda is 0.3; increasing beam width beyond roughly 32 yields diminishing word-error-rate improvement on most benchmarks while increasing decoding latency close to linearly, which is why the reference deployment configuration caps beam width well below the point where further search would meaningfully help.

title: International Acoustic Modeling Research
text: Formantis's Conformer-transducer configuration draws on acoustic modeling research published outside the English-language speech-recognition literature most engineers cite first. Yamada and Kitano's 会話音声認識のための畳み込みと自己注意機構を組み合わせたエンコーダ構造の検討 (2020) examined convolution-and-self-attention encoder combinations for conversational speech recognition, work that directly informed Formantis's choice of a Conformer encoder over a pure self-attention alternative. A separate line of Greek-language research, summarized in Papadopoulos's report, "Η συνδυαστική χρήση μοντέλου μεταγραφέα και εξωτερικού γλωσσικού μοντέλου βελτιώνει σημαντικά την ακρίβεια σε συνθήκες θορύβου," examined transducer-and-external-language-model fusion under noisy conditions and directly shaped Formantis's shallow-fusion decoding strategy. Formantis's engineering documentation cites both works in its architecture design appendix.

title: Phoneme Alignment Statistics
text: Formantis's phoneme-alignment output, generated as a byproduct of the transducer decoder's token-level timing, achieves a mean boundary error of 12.4 milliseconds against hand-annotated reference alignments on Formantis's internal alignment benchmark, comparable to purpose-built forced-alignment tools despite phoneme timing not being the model's primary training objective. Alignment accuracy varies by phoneme class: vowels align with a mean boundary error of 9.1 milliseconds, while stop consonants, whose acoustic boundaries are inherently less distinct due to a silent closure phase before release, average 18.7 milliseconds. Licensees using the phoneme-alignment output for pronunciation scoring report that boundary error below roughly 20 milliseconds is sufficient for their scoring models, a threshold Formantis's Standard and Large models both clear comfortably across all phoneme classes tested. The Compact model's alignment accuracy degrades meaningfully, averaging 24.1 milliseconds mean boundary error, making it a poor fit for alignment-sensitive applications despite its adequate word-error-rate performance.

title: Inference Latency by Model Size
text:
Real-time factor, the ratio of processing time to audio duration, varies sharply across Formantis's three model sizes on the reference server-side accelerator.

| Model | Real-time factor | P99 first-token latency |
|---|---|---|
| Compact | 0.04 | 180 ms |
| Standard | 0.11 | 240 ms |
| Large | 0.29 | 410 ms |

A real-time factor below 1.0 means the model processes audio faster than it arrives, leaving headroom for concurrent utterances per accelerator; Formantis's deployment guidance recommends the Standard model as the default choice balancing accuracy against the concurrency a given accelerator budget can support.

title: International Forced-Alignment Research
text: Formantis's phoneme-alignment evaluation methodology draws on forced-alignment research published outside the English-language literature most speech engineers cite first. Fedorova's Точность посегментного выравнивания в моделях распознавания речи на основе трансдьюсера (2021) established the boundary-error evaluation protocol Formantis's internal alignment benchmark still follows, including the practice of reporting boundary error separately by phoneme class rather than as a single pooled figure. A complementary Turkish-language study, Yıldız's "Transdüser tabanlı konuşma tanıma modellerinde ünsüz kapanma evresi hizalama hatasını önemli ölçüde artırır," specifically examined why consonant closure phases inflate alignment error in transducer-based models, directly explaining the stop-consonant gap Formantis's own benchmark reproduces. Both works are cited in Formantis's alignment-evaluation methodology appendix.

title: Training Data Preparation Checklist
text:
Before a new language or domain-adaptation dataset enters Formantis's training pipeline, the data engineering team follows a fixed preparation sequence. 1. Verify audio-transcript pairs for basic alignment sanity, discarding any pair where transcript length is wildly inconsistent with audio duration.
2. Run automated speech-activity detection to trim leading and trailing silence beyond a configured threshold.
3. Normalize transcript text, expanding numerals and abbreviations into their spoken form consistently with the target language's conventions.
4. Deduplicate near-identical utterances above a configured audio-similarity threshold to avoid overweighting repeated recordings.
5. Reserve a held-out evaluation split stratified by speaker to prevent any speaker from appearing in both training and evaluation data. Datasets failing the initial alignment sanity check are returned to the data vendor rather than entering the pipeline with unresolved pairs.

title: Production Decoding Pipeline Checklist
text:
When a licensee integrates Formantis into a production voice pipeline, the integration follows a fixed validation sequence before receiving sign-off for live traffic. 1. Confirm feature extraction output matches Formantis's reference implementation within the specified numerical tolerance on a standard test-audio set.
2. Load the selected model size and verify inference latency meets the licensee's own concurrency and hardware budget.
3. Configure and validate the external language model fusion weight against the licensee's target domain vocabulary.
4. Run the full benchmark suite against a held-out sample of the licensee's own representative audio, not just Formantis's internal benchmark.
5. Confirm streaming partial-hypothesis behavior meets the licensee's latency requirements under realistic network conditions. A licensee whose domain-specific word error rate exceeds their target after this validation is directed to the fine-tuning toolkit rather than cleared for production traffic.

title: Training Corpus Statistics
text: Formantis's English Large model was trained on 340,000 hours of transcribed audio drawn from a mix of licensed conversational, broadcast, and read-speech corpora, alongside 1.2 million hours of weakly labeled audio used in a self-training pass that refined the model using its own high-confidence predictions on unlabeled data. Non-English languages average 82,000 hours of transcribed audio each, ranging from 210,000 hours for the best-resourced language to 19,000 hours for the smallest. Data augmentation, including speed perturbation and simulated reverberation, effectively triples the training corpus's acoustic diversity without requiring additional labeled audio. Training the Large model from a randomly initialized network to full convergence takes approximately 11 days on Formantis's reference training cluster.

title: Production Deployment Statistics
text: As of the 2026 Q1 licensing report, Formantis is deployed in production by 46 licensees spanning voice assistants, call-center transcription, and clinical dictation, processing a combined 2.1 billion minutes of audio monthly across all deployments. The Standard model accounts for 61% of production traffic by volume, Large for 27%, and Compact, used primarily in on-device or bandwidth-constrained deployments, for the remaining 12%. Mean licensee-reported production word error rate across all deployments, which varies from Formantis's internal benchmark since it reflects each licensee's own domain and audio conditions, is 7.4%, notably higher than the internal benchmark figures given the greater acoustic and vocabulary diversity of real production traffic. Licensees using the fine-tuning toolkit for domain adaptation report a mean relative word-error-rate reduction of 22% compared to the unadapted base model on their own domain-specific evaluation sets.

title: History: From Hybrid HMM-DNN to End-to-End Transducer
text: Formantis began in 2017 as a hybrid acoustic model, pairing a deep neural network that produced per-frame phoneme probabilities with a separate hidden Markov model and pronunciation lexicon that handled the temporal alignment and word-sequence modeling the neural network itself did not learn directly. This architecture, standard in the field at the time, required maintaining three largely separate components, the neural acoustic model, the pronunciation lexicon, and a separately trained language model, each of which needed its own engineering attention and could introduce errors at the boundary between components even when each individually performed well. The first major architectural shift, in 2019, moved to a connectionist temporal classification model that let the neural network learn alignment implicitly rather than relying on a separate HMM, simplifying the pipeline considerably but producing hypotheses less naturally suited to true streaming recognition, since CTC's conditional-independence assumption between output frames limited how well it could use its own prior outputs when predicting the next token. The 2021 release adopted a transducer architecture instead, which conditions each output prediction on both the acoustic encoder and the model's own previous output tokens, resolving CTC's conditional-independence limitation and enabling the low-latency streaming behavior current licensees rely on. Choosing the Conformer encoder specifically, replacing an earlier pure self-attention transformer encoder, came in the 2023 release, after internal benchmarking found the transformer encoder's lack of any explicit local-pattern inductive bias left it comparatively data-hungry relative to a convolution-augmented alternative, a gap that mattered more for Formantis's smaller-resourced non-English languages than for English, where training data was comparatively abundant either way. Looking back, the engineering team describes the shift from hybrid HMM-DNN to end-to-end transducer modeling as the single most consequential architectural change in Formantis's history, since it collapsed what had been three separately engineered components into one jointly trained model, and credits that consolidation, more than any single architecture refinement since, with the largest reduction in both word error rate and the ongoing engineering burden of maintaining the pipeline. Engineers who worked through the CTC-to-transducer transition specifically recall it as a harder migration than the later Conformer switch, since it required rebuilding the training pipeline's loss function and the decoder simultaneously rather than swapping one encoder module for another within an otherwise stable surrounding architecture. The pronunciation lexicon that the original hybrid system depended on was retired gradually rather than all at once, remaining in limited use for several low-resource languages until each language's own transducer model had accumulated enough training data to make the lexicon-based fallback unnecessary, a transition the team completed language by language over roughly two years rather than as a single cutover across the whole product.

title: Incident Postmortem: The March 2024 Non-English Model Regression
text: On March 4th, 2024, a routine model update to Formantis's third-best-resourced non-English language, distributed to production licensees as part of a scheduled quarterly release, was found to have regressed word error rate by 3.1 absolute percentage points relative to the previous release, discovered not by Formantis's own pre-release benchmark, which showed a modest improvement, but by a licensee's own production monitoring flagging an unexpected rise in user-reported transcription complaints within days of the update reaching their fleet. Investigation found that the pre-release benchmark for that language had, several releases earlier, drifted to rely more heavily on read-speech recordings than conversational audio, a shift that had occurred gradually as the benchmark dataset was refreshed release over release without anyone deliberately rebalancing its composition against the language's actual production traffic mix, which remained predominantly conversational. The regressed model had been tuned, intentionally or not, in a direction that improved read-speech accuracy at a measurable cost to conversational accuracy, a tradeoff the drifted benchmark was no longer positioned to catch since it increasingly resembled the easier read-speech condition rather than the harder, more representative conversational mix licensees actually served. Formantis's engineering team rolled back the affected licensees to the prior model version within six hours of the licensee's report, and a corrected model, retrained with the benchmark's original conversational-to-read-speech ratio restored, shipped nine days later after passing both the corrected internal benchmark and a direct comparison against representative licensee-provided production audio. The postmortem's central finding was that benchmark composition drift had gone undetected because no process explicitly tracked the benchmark dataset's composition over time, only its aggregate size and overall word-error-rate trend, both of which looked healthy even as the underlying speech-condition mix quietly shifted. The remediation added an automated composition audit to every benchmark refresh, flagging any release whose conversational-to-read-speech ratio, or other tracked composition metrics, drifted beyond a configured tolerance from the language's known production traffic profile, a check now run for all eleven supported languages rather than only the one directly affected by this incident. A secondary recommendation, adopted alongside the composition audit, required that any pre-release benchmark improvement above a certain margin be independently verified against a small sample of real licensee-provided audio before shipping, rather than trusting the internal benchmark figure alone regardless of how large or consistent the reported improvement appeared. The affected licensee's own monitoring dashboard, which had caught the regression days before Formantis's internal processes would have, was cited approvingly in the postmortem as exactly the kind of independent signal the new verification step was meant to formalize rather than continue relying on informally. No comparable composition-drift regression has been reported across any supported language in the two years since the audit was introduced.

title: Support Ticket Resolution: Case 3182
text:
Case 3182, closed in January 2026, was opened by a licensee reporting that Formantis's Standard model appeared to run slower in their production environment than the published latency specification indicated. Support engineers reviewed the licensee's deployment configuration and benchmarked their specific hardware against Formantis's reference accelerator to isolate the discrepancy. Support quoted the published specification sheet verbatim in the case notes:

| Model | Real-time factor | P99 first-token latency |
|---|---|---|
| Compact | 0.04 | 180 ms |
| Standard | 0.18 | 240 ms |
| Large | 0.29 | 410 ms |

A real-time factor below 1.0 means the model processes audio faster than it arrives, leaving headroom for concurrent utterances per accelerator; Formantis's deployment guidance recommends the Standard model as the default choice balancing accuracy against the concurrency a given accelerator budget can support. The licensee's own benchmark, by contrast, had inadvertently included model-loading time in its latency measurement. Once model-loading time was excluded from the licensee's benchmark methodology, their measured real-time factor matched the reference figure closely, and the case was closed with a recommendation to separate loading and steady-state inference timing in future internal benchmarks.

title: Glossary: Real-Time Factor
text: Real-time factor (RTF): the ratio of audio-processing time to the duration of the audio itself, used throughout Formantis's documentation as the primary latency metric for comparing model sizes and deployment configurations. An RTF below 1.0 indicates the model processes audio faster than real time, leaving headroom for either lower end-to-end latency or higher concurrent-utterance throughput per accelerator, depending on how a licensee chooses to use that headroom. As Formantis's own documentation puts it: Real-time factor, the ratio of processing time to audio duration, varies sharply across Formantis's four model sizes on the reference server-side accelerator. That overview sentence is the one most frequently cited in licensee onboarding materials when evaluating whether the highest-accuracy tier fits a given latency budget. RTF should not be confused with first-token latency, a related but distinct metric describing how long a streaming deployment waits before emitting its first partial hypothesis.
